channel.title: Geoinformatics on xpsong
channel.description: Recent content in Geoinformatics on xpsong

item.title: Using social media user attributes to understand human–environment interactions at urban parks
Analysing spatial patterns of the landscape
item.description:
In landscape ecology, we study interactions between organisms and their surrounding environment. The physical landscape is often quantified using “landscape metrics”, for further analyses alongside other sources of data.

My recent lectures are focused on introducing such concepts to architects and designers. While I’ve used FRAGSTATS and other GIS software for teaching in the past, I’ve always struggled with compatibility/licensing issues that just made the whole process really cumbersome.

Recently, I came across this R package called landscapemetrics. I was absolutely blown away by the sheer power of this thing. I always wanted to be able to introduce people to spatial analysis in R, and to show how a seamless, reproducible workflow can make all your problems disappear. This was the tipping point for me… I decided to give it a shot!

This post provides a quick preview of the workshop content. The workshop is meant to be a follow-up lesson to the Introduction to R series I’ll be conducting next semester.

Workshop outline:


  1. Why analyse spatial patterns?

  2. Landscape ecology: Conceptual models

  3. Land cover classification

  4. Landscape metrics


I’ve uploaded the slides and data on Github, so that anyone interested to try out the analysis for themselves will be able to do so. If you’re interested in geospatial analysis, check it out!



Slide deck:

Note: Click on the slides and use arrows to navigate

item.title: Data-driven approaches to understand human interactions with the environment
item.description:
People’s experiences of their surroundings can vary depending on where they are and their personal interests. Today, the internet and geo-located social media have opened up new ways for us to understand these differences. Technologies to interpret text and image content have also been improving, which can be used to assess the effects of the environment on people’s behaviour, sentiment and aesthetic experiences at specific places.

Many studies have assessed the accuracy of geo-located social media as a proxy for outdoor recreation, but fewer have examined how experiences vary according to different groups of people. With such bottom-up sources of data, it is also challenging to ensure proper data representation across different people groups. It is thus important to consider both the effects of ‘place’ and ‘people’ across multiple datasets, and to build integrated workflows that can help improve the reliability of these data-driven approaches.
